value: Headline: WTI settled at $70.10 Tuesday, off $0.65 on the day, as the market unwinds the Hormuz risk premium even while EIA data confirms US crude stocks keep draining hard. Last week's WPSR delivered a 6.1 mb crude draw to 412.1 mb, but the tape now trades post-conflict: with tankers moving through the Strait again, Reuters' latest poll shows analysts dialing down forecasts and the front of the curve leaking lower. The tension this morning is a physically tight barrel fighting a fading geopolitical bid. Crude & products: For the week ending June 19, 2026, the EIA reported crude drawing 6.1 mb to 412.1 mb, driven by 96.1% refinery utilization (down 0.6 pts) and crude inputs of 17.11 mb/d. The headline within the headline sits at Cushing, where stocks fell 1.1 mb to 18.96 mb — already well below the ~25 mb operational floor and deep into tank-bottom territory. Products moved the other way: gasoline built 2.1 mb to 216.3 mb and distillate built 3.0 mb to 106.1 mb, with jet up 1.3 mb to 46.3 mb. The 4-week export/import picture — 4.93 mb/d exports against 5.75 mb/d imports — underscores that runs, not offshore pull, are doing the draining. Refining margins: Cracks remain robust and are the reason utilization is pinned near 96%. The 3-2-1 sits at $56.68, with the distillate crack a commanding $65.78 and gasoline at $52.13; even the jet crack holds $34.96. RBOB settled at $2.9102/gal (+1c) and ULSD at $3.2353/gal (+5c), the diesel strength reflecting tight middle distillate globally. With margins this fat, refiners have every incentive to keep pulling crude — which is precisely what is starving Cushing. Curve structure: WTI holds a backwardated M1–M12 of $3.33 and a modest M1–M2 of $0.29, consistent with a tight prompt barrel and a sub-floor Cushing. Brent's M1–M12 is a shallower $2.25 and its M1–M2 has actually flipped to a slight contango of -$0.07, a softer prompt signal that fits the easing-supply narrative. The Brent–WTI spread has compressed to just $3.34 — narrow enough that the US export arb is weak, meaning barrels increasingly stay home and pressure domestic builds; to re-open the arb and pull crude offshore, WTI would need to cheapen relative to Brent. RBOB and ULSD both carry steep ~$22/bbl M1–M12 backwardation, product structure screaming summer tightness. News & geopolitics: The dominant thread is de-escalation: with Hormuz flows resuming, a Reuters poll shows analysts cutting oil forecasts, and OilPrice even floats a scenario where an Iran supply crunch could paradoxically send oil back below $40 if demand cracks. Yet the EIA has conceded Middle East disruptions ran worse than prior estimates, and the IEA now sees both global supply and demand contracting in 2026 — a murkier balance than the tape's calm suggests. On the US side, record monthly crude output and a shale equity-raising surge cap the upside, while the BP Indiana refinery lockout dragging into a second month keeps a bid under Midwest product margins. India's continued gorging on discounted Russian barrels and reports of a Russian domestic fuel crisis — pump prices past 100 rubles amid refinery outages — round out a picture where supply risk hasn't vanished, just repriced. Vitol's Bahrain chief warning the market may be underpricing risk is the note worth heeding over that first coffee.
